stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 143 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 45 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.28, PaCO2 57 mmHg, and PaO2 53 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 185 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 59 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.28, PaCO2 71 mmHg, and PaO2 67 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 224 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 72 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.27, PaCO2 96 mmHg, and PaO2 92 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 263 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 85 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.28, PaCO2 109 mmHg, and PaO2 105 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 302 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 110 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.27, PaCO2 122 mmHg, and PaO2 118 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 123 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 416 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 136 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 530 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 174 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.27, PaCO2 186 mmHg, and PaO2 182 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 569 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 187 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.28, PaCO2 211 mmHg, and PaO2 207 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 608 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 200 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.27, PaCO2 224 mmHg, and PaO2 220 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 722 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 238 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.27, PaCO2 250 mmHg, and PaO2 246 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.27, PaCO2 262 mmHg, and PaO2 258 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 761 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 251 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 276 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.27, PaCO2 288 mmHg, and PaO2 284 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 875 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 289 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 914 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 302 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 327 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.28, PaCO2 339 mmHg, and PaO2 335 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 1028 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 340 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 1067 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 353 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 378 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.27, PaCO2 390 mmHg, and PaO2 386 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 1181 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 391 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 1220 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 404 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 429 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.28, PaCO2 441 mmHg, and PaO2 437 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 1334 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 442 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 1373 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 455 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 480 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.27, PaCO2 492 mmHg, and PaO2 488 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 1487 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 493 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 1526 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 506 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 531 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.

stem: A client on oxygen has increasing work of breathing and ABGs of pH 7.28, PaCO2 543 mmHg, and PaO2 539 mmHg. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Delay treatment for a repeat ABG.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 1640 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 544 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

rationale: This pattern shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidemia. NIPPV supports ventilation and oxygenation and can prevent further deterioration. More oxygen alone does not correct hypoventilation.
takeaway: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic respiratory failure requires ventilatory support, not just more oxygen.

stem: A client with suspected airborne infection is admitted to the unit. What PPE is required on room entry?

options.text: N95 respirator with appropriate barrier precautions.
No PPE unless aerosol-generating procedures occur.

rationale: Airborne pathogens require respiratory protection that filters small particles, which is why an N95 is required. The other options under-protect staff. Barrier additions depend on contact exposure risk, but respiratory protection is non-negotiable.
takeaway: Airborne disease -> N95 on entry.

stem: A client receiving IV heparin has an aPTT of 1679 seconds. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.

rationale: This aPTT is markedly supratherapeutic, so the infusion must be stopped before additional anticoagulant is delivered. Provider notification and protocol-based adjustment come next. The other options either worsen bleeding risk or use the wrong antidote.
takeaway: Unsafe heparin level -> stop the infusion first.

stem: A client with diabetes has blood glucose 557 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion and is unsafe for oral intake. What is the first action?

options.text: Administer glucagon IM or dextrose IV based on access.
Wait for a repeat glucose check in 15 minutes.
Give regular insulin to control rebound hyperglycemia.

rationale: This is symptomatic hypoglycemia and requires immediate parenteral rescue because oral intake is unsafe. Delaying treatment risks seizure and coma. Insulin would worsen the emergency.
takeaway: Hypoglycemia without safe swallowing -> treat immediately with glucagon or IV dextrose.

stem: Which task is appropriate for the nurse to delegate to a UAP?

options.text: Obtain routine vital signs on a stable client.

rationale: Routine, predictable tasks for stable clients can be delegated. Assessment, teaching, and interpretation remain RN responsibilities. The correct option requires no clinical judgment.
takeaway: Delegate stable routine work, not assessment or teaching.
